verses.book: A Commentary on the Apostles' Creed.
verses.text: But they say, perhaps, If it was possible to God that a virgin should conceive, it was possible also that she should bring forth, but they think it unmeet that a being of so great majesty should enter the world in such wise, that even though there had been no defilement from intercourse with man, there should yet be the unseemliness attendant upon the act of delivery.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Apostles' Creed.
verses.text: To which let us reply briefly, meeting them on their own level.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Apostles' Creed.
verses.text: If a person should see a little child in the act of being suffocated in a quagmire, and himself, a great man and powerful, should go into the mire, just at its verge, so to say, to rescue the dying child; would you blame this man as defiled for having stepped into a little mire, or would you praise him as merciful, for having preserved the life of one that was perishing?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Apostles' Creed.
verses.text: But the case supposed is that of an ordinary man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Apostles' Creed.
verses.text: Let us return to the nature of Him Who was born.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Apostles' Creed.
verses.text: How much, think you, is the nature of the Sun inferior to him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Apostles' Creed.
verses.text: How much beyond doubt, the Creature to the Creator?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Apostles' Creed.
verses.text: Consider now if a ray of the sun alights upon a quagmire, does it receive any pollution from it? or is the sun the worse for shedding his light upon foul objects?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Apostles' Creed.
verses.text: Fire, too, how far inferior is its nature to the things of which we are speaking?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Apostles' Creed.
verses.text: Yet no substance, whether foul or vile, is believed to pollute fire if applied to it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Apostles' Creed.
verses.text: When the case is plainly thus with regard to material things, do you suppose that aught of pollution and defilement can befall that supereminent and incorporeal nature, which is above all fire and all light?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Apostles' Creed.
verses.text: Then, lastly, note this also: we say that man was created by God out of the clay of the earth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Apostles' Creed.
verses.text: But if God is thought to be defiled in seeking to recover His own work, much more must He be thought so in making that work originally.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Apostles' Creed.
verses.text: And it is idle to ask why He passed through what is repugnant to our sense of modesty, when you cannot tell why He made what is so repugnant.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Apostles' Creed.
verses.text: And therefore it is not nature but general estimation that has made us think these things to be such.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Apostles' Creed.
verses.text: Otherwise, all things that are in the body, being formed from one and the same clay, are distinguished from one another only in their uses and natural offices.
